text: A sixty-five-year-old man with a history of previous pulmonary embolism has symptoms of vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, and high fever.
A CT scan showed an avascular, twenty-centimeter, tract of proximal jejuno and portal thrombosis.
The patient underwent a laparoscopy in urgency.
Following port placement, the surgeon explores the abdominal cavity, inspecting the internal structures for any abnormalities or signs of disease.
In the left upper quadrant of the abdomen, several proximal jejunal loops are visibly fused together.
During visceral lysis, a gangrenous loop of jejunum is revealed, located twenty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon retracts the mesentery of the gangrenous jejunal loop, which appears thickened, likely due to mesenteric venous microthrombosis.
The surgeon uses a GIE stapler to section the proximal jejunum in a portion of vital tissue, located twenty centimeters away from the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector and metal clips to section the mesentery, visibly separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the necrotic jejunal loop, taking care to avoid injuring the adjacent transverse colon and its vascular structures.
The surgeon continues to section the mesentery using an ultrasonic dissector, focusing on the area surrounding the gangrenous jejunal loop.
The surgeon releases the necrotic intestinal loop, measuring approximately twenty centimeters, and proceeds to section the distal part.
The surgeon recreates intestinal continuity by performing an antiperistaltic side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis using a GIA stapler.
The surgeon closes the enterotomy using a double layer of T-zero absorbable running suture, ensuring the incision is securely sealed.
The surgeon uses a running absorbable suture to secure the proximal jejunal stump, and then covers the anastomosis with the omentum.
Operative time was one hundred sixteen minutes without blood loss.
The scourge was in the fifth postoperative day without complication and the patient was entrusted to the thrombosis center for more accurate hematochemical screening.
